turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion in case 21-468, National Pork Producers Council versus Ross.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: This case involves pigs, more exactly it involves a California law known as Proposition 12 that prohibits the sale of pork in California grocery stores derived from pigs who were forced to live their lives in cages so small they cannot turn around. A few years ago, California voters adopted Proposition 12 by a wide margin. Almost immediately two groups of out-of-state pork producers responded by filing this lawsuit. They argued that Proposition 12 unconstitutionally interferes with their preferred methods of production. More specifically, they pointed to this Court's decisions applying what some call the dormant aspect of the Constitution's Interstate Commerce Clause. Both the district court and the Ninth Circuit dismissed the producers' suit. After that, we agreed to hear the case for ourselves. Today, we hold that at their core, the Court's dormant Commerce Clause decisions stand for the principle and only the principle that a state cannot adopt laws that purposefully discriminate against out-of-state businesses, and we hold nothing in Proposition 12 offends that principle. In fact, even the producers appear to concede that Proposition 12 applies equally to in-state and out-of-state pork producers alike. Recognizing as much, the producers invite us to use this case as a vehicle to fashion two new and far more expansive theories of dormant Commerce Clause liability. Today, we decline both invitations. First, the producers asked us to adopt a rule prohibiting any state from adopting a law that carries with it some extraterritorial effect. The Court unanimously rejects that proposal. From the founding to the present, states have adopted all kinds of regulations that bear consequences extending beyond their borders. Think of state income tax laws that lead some individuals and companies to relocate to other jurisdictions, or commercial and environmental laws that often proved decisive when businesses choose where to incorporate or manufacture their goods. The list goes on. And no one has ever supposed that all of those laws violate the constitution. Second, the producers ask us to weigh for ourselves the benefits and burdens Proposition 12 will have on interstate commerce, and because the burdens of this law outweigh its benefits, they say, we should allow their case to proceed. The producers argue that this Court's 1970 decision in Pike versus Bruce Church requires exactly that. Today, a majority of the Court rejects this argument too. As an initial matter, the majority explains that the producers over read Pike. Generally speaking Pike simply authorizes courts to examine a law's effects in order to determine whether it purposefully discriminates against out-of-state firms or directly burdens instrumentalities of interstate commerce, like trucks and trains. In that way, Pike is just one more manifestation of this Court's usual in a discrimination principle. Of course, the producers argue that Pike does much more than that. They argue that it requires us to overturn the Ninth Circuit's decision against them and allow their case to proceed. But members of the majority disagree with this assessment for two distinct reasons. First, Justice Thomas, Justice Barrett and I reject the producers. suggestion that Pike authorizes us to weigh for ourselves the benefits and burdens of Proposition 12. Doing that, we hold, would be inconsistent with the judicial function. We are in no better position than voters to decide about this law.s costs and benefits, and the Constitution.s protections for interstate commerce do not provide courts with a roving license to decide what activities are appropriate for state governments to undertake. Second, Justice Thomas, Justice Sotomayor, Justice Kagan and I  conclude that even under the kind of balancing test the producers proposed an associate with Pike, their claim would still fail. It would fail because the producers do not plausibly allege the Proposition 12 imposes a substantial burden on interstate commerce. To be sure, some out-of-state producers who see profit in serving the California market will now have to change their production methods and allow pigs more space than they may prefer. But other out-of-state producers tell us they are already raising pigs and compliance with Proposition 12. In this Court's dormant Commerce Clause cases have never been used to shield one set of producers favorite methods of production nor do we believe our doctrine should be used that way now. The judgment of the Ninth Circuit is affirmed. Justice Sotomayor has written a concurring opinion joined by Justice Kagan. Justice Barrett has written a concurring opinion. Chief Justice Roberts has written an opinion concurring in part and descending in part. He is joined by Justice Alito, Justice Kavanaugh, and Justice Jackson. Justice Kavanaugh has also written a concurring opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part.
